title: The connection between irritable bowel and unprocessed anger
author: Kalesh

htmlContent:
The Gut That Holds Your Rage

I spent years thinking my gut was just broken. Randomly, without warning, my stomach would clench into a knot. Cramps would double me over. I'd cancel plans, leave dinners early, spend hours on the bathroom floor wondering what I'd eaten wrong.

I tried elimination diets. Cut out dairy, gluten, sugar, caffeine. I tried probiotics, enzymes, fiber supplements. Nothing stuck. The flare-ups kept coming, unpredictable and brutal, like my intestines had a mind of their own.

Here's the thing. My gut wasn't broken. It was talking. It was screaming, actually, in the only language it knew. And what it was trying to say was something I'd spent decades refusing to hear.

I was angry. Furious, really. But I'd learned somewhere along the way that anger wasn't safe. That nice people don't get angry. That if I felt rage, I should swallow it down, smile, and move on.

So I swallowed. For years. Decades. And my gut swallowed too, holding every unspoken word, every clenched jaw, every moment I wanted to scream but stayed silent. It held all of it, right there in my digestive tract, until the holding became a kind of slow fire.

Irritable bowel syndrome, or IBS, affects roughly 10 to 15 percent of people globally. That's hundreds of millions of us walking around with guts that feel like they're at war. And while the medical establishment has focused on diet, microbiome, motility, and pharmaceuticals, there's a piece of the puzzle that rarely gets mentioned in a doctor's office.

Unprocessed anger. The kind you don't even know you're carrying. The kind that lives in your tissues, in your fascia, in the smooth muscle of your intestines. The kind that has nowhere to go but inward.

Stay with me here. This isn't about blaming yourself for your symptoms. It's not another thing to add to your shame pile. It's an invitation to look at something that might actually help.

The Science of Suppression

Stephen Porges, the neuroscientist who developed polyvagal theory, spent decades mapping how our nervous system responds to threat. His work shows that when we perceive danger - and emotional danger counts - our body shifts into protective states. We freeze. We fawn. We collapse. We disconnect from our gut sensations because feeling them would mean feeling the fear, or the rage, that we couldn't safely express.

Think about it. When you were a child and someone hurt you, what happened when you got angry? Maybe you were punished. Maybe you were shamed. Maybe you were told you were too sensitive, too dramatic, too much. So you learned to shut it down. You learned that anger was dangerous, that expressing it could get you rejected, abandoned, hurt even more.

So you did what any smart kid would do. You pushed the anger down. You tightened your throat, your chest, your belly. You stopped the feeling from moving through you. And your gut, being the sensitive, intelligent organ it is, took the hit.

Bessel van der Kolk, author of "The Body Keeps the Score," has shown that trauma isn't just stored in our memories. It's stored in our bodies. In our muscles. In our organs. In the very tissues that process our food and our emotions alike. He writes about how the body's response to threat can become chronic, stuck on repeat, even after the danger is long gone.

So here's what happens. You experience something that triggers anger. Maybe your boss dismisses your work. Maybe your partner ignores your needs. Maybe someone cuts you off in traffic. Your nervous system activates, preparing you to fight. Your gut slows down or speeds up, depending on the threat. Blood moves away from digestion toward your muscles. Your stomach clenches.

But you don't fight. You don't even feel the anger. You just feel your gut cramp. You just feel nauseous. You just feel the urgent need to find a bathroom. The anger has been rerouted, like a river diverted underground, and it surfaces as a physical symptom instead of an emotion.

Wild, right? Your body is doing exactly what it learned to do to keep you safe. It's just that the strategy is no longer serving you. It's making you sick.

The Body Remembers What You Forgot

Something worth exploring for those drawn to body-based healing is In an Unspoken Voice (paid link) by Peter Levine. Levine is the founder of Somatic Experiencing, and his work is built on the idea that trauma lives in the body's incomplete responses to threat. When we can't complete the fight response - when we can't actually express the anger - the energy stays trapped.

I remember the first time I tried to feel my anger on purpose. I was lying on my yoga mat, trying to do a body scan, and my therapist said, "Just notice what's there." What was there was a knot in my upper stomach, tight and hot. I tried to breathe into it. It got tighter. I tried to relax. It got tighter still.

Then she said something that changed everything. "What does that knot want to do?"

I almost laughed. What does a knot want to do? But I stayed with it, and the answer came from somewhere deep. It wants to push. It wants to shove. It wants to punch. It wants to scream.

That knot wasn't just tension. It was years of unexpressed rage, held in the precise location where my small intestine meets my stomach. The exact spot where my IBS symptoms always started.

I'm not saying that all IBS is caused by unprocessed anger. That would be reductive and dismissive of the real physical complexity of this condition. But I am saying that for many of us, anger is a major piece of the puzzle. And ignoring it means we keep treating symptoms without addressing the root.

A book that connects the dots between stress and illness is When the Body Says No (paid link) by Gabor Mate. Mate's work explores how suppressed emotions, particularly anger, contribute to chronic illness. He argues that the body literally says no when we can't say it ourselves. The gut is one of the most common places this shows up.

Learning to Feel the Fire

So what do you do with all this swallowed rage? The answer isn't to go punch walls or scream at strangers. That's discharge, not processing. True processing means letting the anger move through you without acting on it, without judging it, without trying to make it go away.

Here's a practice that helped me. Next time you feel that familiar gut cramp, the one that usually sends you running for the bathroom or the antacids, stop. Don't try to fix it. Don't try to breathe it away. Just notice it. Notice the quality of the sensation. Is it sharp or dull? Is it hot or cold? Does it have a color? A shape?

Then ask yourself, without forcing an answer: Is there any anger here? Any frustration? Any irritation? Any sense of being wronged or dismissed or unseen? You might not get an answer right away. That's okay. The body doesn't always trust us with its secrets on the first try.

But over time, as you practice this kind of curious attention, the connection between your gut and your emotions will become clearer. You'll start to notice patterns. The cramp that shows up right after a difficult conversation. The nausea that comes when you're about to set a boundary. The bloating that follows a day of people-pleasing.

A practical guide to working with your nervous system is Anchored (paid link) by Deb Dana. Dana is a leading clinician in polyvagal theory, and her book offers concrete exercises for shifting out of protective states and into connection. It's not about eliminating anger. It's about giving your nervous system enough safety to let the anger move.

I'm not going to tell you that processing your anger will cure your IBS. I don't know that. And I'm suspicious of anyone who promises a simple fix for something as complex as a chronic condition. But I will tell you that for me, learning to feel my anger was the first thing that actually made a difference. The cramps didn't disappear overnight. But they changed. They became less frequent, less intense, less terrifying.

More , I stopped being afraid of my own gut. I stopped treating it as an enemy that was betraying me. I started seeing it as a messenger, carrying news from parts of myself I'd been ignoring for too long.

Sit with that. What if your IBS isn't a malfunction? What if it's a form of communication? What if your gut is trying to tell you something that your conscious mind has been unwilling to hear?

That's a hard question. It might make you angry. And that's exactly the point.

Healing Journey Resources

  • In an Unspoken Voice (paid link) - Peter Levine's exploration of how the body holds and releases trauma
  • When the Body Says No (paid link) - Gabor Mate's research on the connection between suppressed emotion and illness
  • Anchored (paid link) - Deb Dana's practical polyvagal guide for nervous system regulation
  • The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy (paid link) - Deb Dana's clinical resource for applying polyvagal principles in healing work
